name: Gabardito Refuge Loop
name_original: Refugio Forestal de Gabardito – circular de trail running desde Canfranc
description: This difficult 5.4-mile (8.7 km) trail running circular from Canfranc to the Forest Refuge of Gabardito offers challenging terrain and Pyren
region_intro: Jogging routes around Canfranc are set within the Spanish Pyrenees, characterized by a varied and challenging topography. The region features steep ascents and descents, with trails traversing open pastures, loose rocks, and dense forest paths. The Aragon River valley, limestone outcrops, and numerous waterfalls contribute to the diverse natural environment. This mountainous setting provides a robust backdrop for trail running and jogging. Best jogging routes around Canfranc The most popular jogging route is Gabardito Refuge Loop, a 5.4 miles (8.7 km) trail that takes 1 hour 37 minutes to complete, offering significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local runners is Canfranc Estación – Canfranc Estación loop from Canfranc, a difficult 17.0 miles (27.4 km) path. This route features extensive climbs and descents, showcasing the rugged Pyrenean landscape. Local runners also love the Canal Roya – Ibones de Anayet loop from Canfranc, a 17.2 miles (27.6 km) trail leading through high mountain valleys and past glacial lakes, often completed in about 4 hours 37 minutes. Jogging around Canfranc is defined by high Pyrenean peaks, the Aragon River valley, and dense forests, with options ranging from challenging mountain trails to more moderate paths. The network offers routes for different ability levels, from experienced trail runners to those seeking a demanding workout. The routes in Canfranc are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 1,700 runners have used komoot to explore Canfranc's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Canfranc?
A: Canfranc offers a diverse selection of over 20 running routes, catering to various fitness levels. You'll find everything from moderate paths to highly challenging mountain trails, all set within the stunning Pyrenean landscape.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails in Canfranc?
A: The terrain in Canfranc is notably varied and challenging, characterized by the Pyrenees' hard topography. Runners will encounter steep ascents and descents, traversing open pastures, loose rocks, mud, roots, dense forest trails, and mountain roads. This diversity makes it an exceptional destination for trail running.

Q: Are there any easy running routes for beginners in Canfranc?
A: While many routes in Canfranc are challenging, there is at least one easy running route available. The region offers options for different ability levels, ensuring that even those new to trail running can find a suitable path to enjoy the scenery.

Q: Can I find challenging trail running options in Canfranc?
A: Absolutely. Canfranc is renowned for its challenging trail running. The majority of routes, 15 out of 22, are classified as difficult. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20524215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canfranc Estación – Canfranc Estación loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 17.0-mile (27.4 km) path with extensive climbs and descents, showcasing the rugged Pyrenean landscape.

Q: Are there running routes that feature waterfalls or other natural landmarks?
A: Yes, Canfranc's natural beauty includes several stunning features. You can discover beautiful waterfalls like Cascada de Canfranc Pueblo and Cascada de las Negras. The region also boasts impressive river gorges such as Ip, Aguare, Curuye, and Los Meses, and lakes like Lac Roumassot, all of which can be incorporated into scenic running experiences.

Q: What historical or architectural sights can I see while running in Canfranc?
A: Canfranc offers a rich blend of natural beauty and historical landmarks. You might pass by the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5129372" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal Roya&lt;/a&gt; gorge, or the historic Somport Pass, a key route on the Camino de Santiago. The iconic Canfranc International Station, an architectural marvel, is also a prominent feature in the area, and the medieval quarter offers glimpses into the past.

Q: Are there circular running routes available in Canfranc?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Canfranc are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949680090/gabardito-refuge-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gabardito Refuge Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.4-mile (8.7 km) trail offering significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in Canfranc?
A: Canfranc is excellent for running throughout much of the year, but the best time often depends on your preference for weather and trail conditions. Spring and autumn offer milder temperatures and vibrant scenery, while summer provides longer daylight hours. Winter running is possible but may require specific gear due to snow and ice at higher elevations.

Q: Is Canfranc a good destination for dog-friendly running?
A: Canfranc's extensive network of trails and natural environment generally makes it a great place for running with dogs. However, it's always recommended to check specific trail regulations and local guidelines, especially in protected areas or during certain seasons, to ensure a safe and enjoyable experience for both you and your canine companion.

Q: Are there family-friendly running paths in Canfranc?
A: While many routes are challenging, Canfranc does offer some more moderate paths that can be suitable for families looking for a less strenuous running experience. These paths often follow the Aragon River valley or explore the lower forest trails, providing beautiful scenery without extreme elevation changes. Always check the route difficulty before heading out with family.

Q: How do other runners rate the trails in Canfranc?
A: The running routes in Canfranc are highly rated by the komoot community, with an impressive average score of 4.9 stars from more than 5 reviews. Over 1,700 runners have used komoot to explore Canfranc's varied terrain, often praising the stunning Pyrenean landscape and the challenging yet rewarding trails.

Q: Are there running routes that offer views of glacial lakes?
A: Yes, you can find running routes that lead to breathtaking glacial lakes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20524221" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal Roya – Ibones de Anayet loop from Canfranc&lt;/a&gt; is a 17.2-mile (27.6 km) trail that takes you through high mountain valleys and past these beautiful glacial lakes, offering spectacular views.

Q: Can I combine running with exploring the Camino de Santiago in Canfranc?
A: Canfranc is historically significant as it lies on the Somport Pass, a key route on the Camino de Santiago. While the Camino is primarily a walking pilgrimage, sections of it, particularly those around Canfranc, can be incorporated into longer running routes, allowing you to experience both the physical challenge and the historical essence of the path.
tips: Shelter not guarded but open and reasonably clean. Approximately one hour from Canfranc, . There is no water in it. Magnificent views to the North with the Lecherines, Pico Tortiellas and Pico Lecherín and to the East towards the Ip Valley and the Collarada massif.

There are numerous pilgrims who pass through the town every year following the Aragonese section of the Camino Frances de Santiago. This route lived its splendor in the Middle Ages, five centuries of pilgrimage that left wonderful stories and legends, as well as an artistic and cultural legacy of extraordinary richness.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is rated as difficult and is not suitable for beginners. It's described as a 'Hard run' requiring 'very good fitness,' 'sure-footedness, sturdy shoes, and alpine experience.' The route includes a 'quite steep' climb with a 'very technical' section involving 'clavijas y cadenas' (pegs and chains).

faqs.question: Where does the trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail starts from Canfranc-Pueblo. While specific parking details aren't provided, Canfranc is a significant hub for outdoor activities, so parking options are generally available within the village. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is incredibly varied and challenging. It begins with a 'quite steep' climb that includes a 'very technical' rocky section equipped with 'clavijas y cadenas' (pegs and chains). After this, the path becomes 'quite comfortable' but maintains a significant incline. You can expect a mix of open pastures, loose rocks, mud, roots, forest trails, mountain roads, and potentially karst sections.

faqs.question: What notable features or viewpoints will I encounter?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll experience breathtaking Pyrenean views. Key features include the Gabardito meadow (pradera de Gabardito), wild rose bushes, and various forest areas. The trail leads to the Refugio de Gabardito, and you'll pass by the Fuente de la Pajeta (Pajeta Fountain) and Barranco de Los Meses (Los Meses Ravine). The starting point is near the Church of the Assumption of Canfranc.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the 'alpine experience' requirement and the 'hard topography' of the Aragonese Pyrenees, the best time to run this trail is typically during late spring, summer, and early autumn. Winter conditions can be severe, requiring specialized gear and expertise due to snow and ice. Always check local weather and trail conditions before heading out.

faqs.question: Is the Forest Refuge of Gabardito trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many trails in the Pyrenees allow dogs, specific regulations for this particular route are not detailed. Given the technical sections with 'clavijas y cadenas' and potentially challenging terrain, it might not be suitable for all dogs. If you bring your dog, ensure they are well-behaved, on a leash, and capable of handling difficult mountain terrain. Always practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is located in the Aragonese Pyrenees. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for trail running on public paths in this region. However, it's always good practice to check for any local regulations or protected area rules that might apply, especially if you plan to deviate from the main route or visit specific natural parks.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the "Variante Tacheras – Oza – Ref. Gabardito – Ref. de Lizara – Canfranc Estación – Canal Roya" and the "Pista de Villanua a Gabardito". You'll also find smaller segments overlapping with the "Aragonese Way" and the "Camino de Santiago".

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or accommodation near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail starts from Canfranc-Pueblo, which is a known hub for trail running and tourism. You can expect to find amenities such as cafes, pubs, and accommodation options in Canfranc to support your visit before or after your run.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this trail run?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' rating and varied terrain, you should bring sturdy trail running shoes with good grip, appropriate clothing layers for changing mountain weather, sufficient water and snacks, a first-aid kit, and a map or GPS device. Due to the technical sections, gloves might be useful for the chains and pegs. 'Alpine experience' is recommended, implying preparedness for mountain conditions.
